category_summary:
**OpenAI's** military and surveillance dealings dominated the discourse. **Jeremy Howard** [published a legal analysis](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-ff74b7306fff) arguing **OpenAI's** claim that its Department of War contract locks in current law is almost certainly incorrect, citing 150 years of Supreme Court precedent. **Gary Marcus** [amplified concerns](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-ce56a028fc2c) about **OpenAI** agreeing to surveillance-enabling laws, and a viral internal quote — *'If you stay at OpenAI, you're on the wrong side of history'* — fueled the growing **#DeleteChatGPT** movement.

- **Ethan Mollick** went viral with a [satirical template](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-c13ca3d7a169) exposing formulaic AI-generated writing, while also observing that 'everyone speaks **Claude** now' — highlighting AI content homogeneity as a cultural phenomenon
- **Jerry Liu** (**LlamaIndex** CEO) delivered a [technical explainer](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-cfcced41c39b) on why PDF parsing is fundamentally hard, earning massive engagement (1,035 likes, 139K views)
- **Google's** **Logan Kilpatrick** announced **Gemini 3 Pro** deprecation on March 9, urging migration to **Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview**
- **Mollick** [shared empirical evidence](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-72ed8bfeb038) that major Chinese open-weight models are fragile on out-of-distribution tasks despite strong benchmark scores
- **Boris Cherny** (**Cursor** CEO) [publicly addressed criticism](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-a60dcae6f06c), attributing issues to 10x year-over-year user growth rather than engineering failures
- Reports emerged that **Anthropic's Claude** is [being purged](/?date=2026-03-03&category=social#item-0a26d3836bbc) from U.S. government use, contrasting sharply with **OpenAI's** expanding Pentagon partnerships
category_summary_html:
OpenAI's military and surveillance dealings dominated the discourse. Jeremy Howard published a legal analysis arguing OpenAI's claim that its Department of War contract locks in current law is almost certainly incorrect, citing 150 years of Supreme Court precedent. Gary Marcus amplified concerns about OpenAI agreeing to surveillance-enabling laws, and a viral internal quote — *'If you stay at OpenAI, you're on the wrong side of history'* — fueled the growing #DeleteChatGPT movement.

  • Ethan Mollick went viral with a satirical template exposing formulaic AI-generated writing, while also observing that 'everyone speaks Claude now' — highlighting AI content homogeneity as a cultural phenomenon
  • Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) delivered a technical explainer on why PDF parsing is fundamentally hard, earning massive engagement (1,035 likes, 139K views)
  • Google's Logan Kilpatrick announced Gemini 3 Pro deprecation on March 9, urging migration to Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview
  • Mollick shared empirical evidence that major Chinese open-weight models are fragile on out-of-distribution tasks despite strong benchmark scores
  • Boris Cherny (Cursor CEO) publicly addressed criticism, attributing issues to 10x year-over-year user growth rather than engineering failures
  • Reports emerged that Anthropic's Claude is being purged from U.S. government use, contrasting sharply with OpenAI's expanding Pentagon partnerships

items.title: The fundamental issue with PDF parsing is that PDFs are designed for display purposes. The internal ...
items.content:
The fundamental issue with PDF parsing is that PDFs are designed for display purposes. The internal representation of data is outputting shapes at specific coordinates on the page (e.g. "render this string at coordinate (84, 720) with this font")

each displayed character could be not contiguous at all, there could be no font mapping back to unicode so you have no idea what the character is. Any PDF parser needs to magically reconstruct this random sequence of display coordinate data into semantically meaningful text, tables, and more. 

VLMs do help (screenshot the page and read it), but besides collapsing the metadata they still struggle in terms of accuracy and cost.

note: parsing Word/Pptx as text representations so typically a bit easier too read. 

Our entire company at @llama_index is laser-focused on PDF parsing so we've been really trying to understand all the nuances of doc formats, especially PDFs 🙂 more notes on this coming soon
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) provides deep technical explanation of why PDF parsing is fundamentally hard - PDFs store display coordinates not semantic structure, making text reconstruction challenging. Notes VLMs help but struggle with accuracy/cost. Says LlamaIndex is focused on this.
items.reasoning: Excellent technical explainer from a domain expert. Massive engagement (1,035 likes, 139K views). Provides genuine insight into a widely-encountered problem in AI/RAG pipelines.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) provides deep technical explanation of why PDF parsing is fundamentally hard - PDFs store display coordinates not semantic structure, making text reconstruction challenging. Notes VLMs help but struggle with accuracy/cost. Says LlamaIndex is focused on this.
items.content_html:
The fundamental issue with PDF parsing is that PDFs are designed for display purposes. The internal representation of data is outputting shapes at specific coordinates on the page (e.g. "render this string at coordinate (84, 720) with this font")

each displayed character could be not contiguous at all, there could be no font mapping back to unicode so you have no idea what the character is. Any PDF parser needs to magically reconstruct this random sequence of display coordinate data into semantically meaningful text, tables, and more.

VLMs do help (screenshot the page and read it), but besides collapsing the metadata they still struggle in terms of accuracy and cost.

note: parsing Word/Pptx as text representations so typically a bit easier too read.

Our entire company at @llama_index is laser-focused on PDF parsing so we've been really trying to understand all the nuances of doc formats, especially PDFs 🙂 more notes on this coming soon

items.title:
Claude Code tips:

(Add these to your CLAUDE .md file)

1. Before writing any code, describe your ap...
items.content:
Claude Code tips:

(Add these to your CLAUDE .md file)

1. Before writing any code, describe your approach and wait for approval.

2. If the requirements I give you are ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before writing any code.

3. After you finish writing any code, list the edge cases and suggest test cases to cover them.

4. If a task requires changes to more than 3 files, stop and break it into smaller tasks first.

5. When there’s a bug, start by writing a test that reproduces it, then fix it until the test passes.

6. Every time I correct you, reflect on what you did wrong and come up with a plan to never make the same mistake again.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago shares 6 practical Claude Code tips for CLAUDE.md files: requiring approach approval, clarifying questions, edge case testing, limiting multi-file changes, bug reproduction via tests, and self-reflection on mistakes.
items.reasoning: Highly practical and viral (1,206 likes, 100K+ views). Actionable prompt engineering tips for Claude Code that many developers will adopt. High engagement signals strong community interest.
items.summary_html: Santiago shares 6 practical Claude Code tips for CLAUDE.md files: requiring approach approval, clarifying questions, edge case testing, limiting multi-file changes, bug reproduction via tests, and self-reflection on mistakes.
items.content_html:
Claude Code tips:

(Add these to your CLAUDE .md file)

1. Before writing any code, describe your approach and wait for approval.

2. If the requirements I give you are ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before writing any code.

3. After you finish writing any code, list the edge cases and suggest test cases to cover them.

4. If a task requires changes to more than 3 files, stop and break it into smaller tasks first.

5. When there’s a bug, start by writing a test that reproduces it, then fix it until the test passes.

6. Every time I correct you, reflect on what you did wrong and come up with a plan to never make the same mistake again.

if you read The Agent Labs Thesis, custom model trai...
items.content:
# unconstrained Opus-tier koding at ~1000tok/s

if you read The Agent Labs Thesis, custom model training is the smallest part of what an ideal Agent Lab should do. i was initially a skeptic that cog should invest a lot more in swe-x other than as a defensive manuever - cant out-bitter-lesson the big labs, but for “most” or routing tasks you could still do it.

I’ve genuinely been turned around on this. the Cognition for Governments business is scaling to billions in ARR. and having own model based on being the first and largest cloud coding agents biz is offense, not defense.

# unconstrained Opus-tier koding at ~1000tok/s

if you read The Agent Labs Thesis, custom model training is the smallest part of what an ideal Agent Lab should do. i was initially a skeptic that cog should invest a lot more in swe-x other than as a defensive manuever - cant out-bitter-lesson the big labs, but for “most” or routing tasks you could still do it.

I’ve genuinely been turned around on this. the Cognition for Governments business is scaling to billions in ARR. and having own model based on being the first and largest cloud coding agents biz is offense, not defense.

items.title:
I want to move permanently to Claude, but their desktop experience is nowhere near ChatGPT.

You can...
items.content:
I want to move permanently to Claude, but their desktop experience is nowhere near ChatGPT.

You can’t upload large/multiple documents to Claude because it can’t process them. ChatGPT ingests whatever you throw at it.

This is the only thing keeping me from canceling my ChatGPT subscription.

items.title: Multiple independent legal doctrines, spanning 150 years of Supreme Court precedent and the foundati...

items.title: “No matter what world we build, the limitations imposed in the law on what we know today as “the gov...
items.content: “No matter what world we build, the limitations imposed in the law on what we know today as “the government’s” use of AI will be of paramount importance. We really do want to ensure that mass surveillance and autonomous weapons/systems of control cannot be used to curtail our liberties—at least we want to try. So despite not being the focus of this piece, I applaud the AI labs for caring about these redlines. Over the coming years and decades, I expect that our liberty will be in greater peril than many of us comprehend.”
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus shares a quote about the importance of AI governance — specifically ensuring mass surveillance and autonomous weapons cannot curtail liberties, while acknowledging AI labs' red lines.
items.reasoning: Important AI governance topic. The quote highlights tension between government AI use and civil liberties. Moderate engagement. Relevant given OpenAI compliance news.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus shares a quote about the importance of AI governance — specifically ensuring mass surveillance and autonomous weapons cannot curtail liberties, while acknowledging AI labs' red lines.
items.content_html: “No matter what world we build, the limitations imposed in the law on what we know today as “the government’s” use of AI will be of paramount importance. We really do want to ensure that mass surveillance and autonomous weapons/systems of control cannot be used to curtail our liberties—at least we want to try. So despite not being the focus of this piece, I applaud the AI labs for caring about these redlines. Over the coming years and decades, I expect that our liberty will be in greater peril than many of us comprehend.”

items.title:
This is also true of contracts with the government.

In Winstar (1996) the Supreme Court held 7-2 th...
items.content:
This is also true of contracts with the government.

In Winstar (1996) the Supreme Court held 7-2 that a clause referring to "all applicable statutes" required obeying future laws as they arose; it did *not* freeze the regulatory framework at the time of signing. https://t.co/N8SyOCU4WQ

items.title: we are finalizing @aidotengineer Europe speaker acceptances and realizing that we actually dont have...
items.content:
we are finalizing @aidotengineer Europe speaker acceptances and realizing that we actually dont have enough good speakers for these tracks:

- mechanistic interpretability
- generative media (video/image but could also use more voice/music)
- Claw track (openclaw etc power users)

if you have GOOD work - not an ad for your barely launched startup, actual good work - in these areas please please reply or DM and I’ll skip you to the front of the line. We pay all travel and accommodation for our international speakers, and our talks regularly get hundreds of thousands of views. 

https://t.co/e8jVB7JgE9
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: Swyx seeks speakers for ai.engineer Europe in mechanistic interpretability, generative media, and 'Claw track' (Claude power users). Offers travel/accommodation and notes talks get hundreds of thousands of views.
items.reasoning: Signals which AI topics are undersupplied with quality speakers: mech interp, generative media, and Claude power usage. Good community engagement (123 likes, 15K views). Also interesting signal about a 'Claw track' for Claude power users.
items.summary_html: Swyx seeks speakers for ai.engineer Europe in mechanistic interpretability, generative media, and 'Claw track' (Claude power users). Offers travel/accommodation and notes talks get hundreds of thousands of views.
items.content_html:
we are finalizing @aidotengineer Europe speaker acceptances and realizing that we actually dont have enough good speakers for these tracks:

  • mechanistic interpretability
  • generative media (video/image but could also use more voice/music)
  • Claw track (openclaw etc power users)

if you have GOOD work - not an ad for your barely launched startup, actual good work - in these areas please please reply or DM and I’ll skip you to the front of the line. We pay all travel and accommodation for our international speakers, and our talks regularly get hundreds of thousands of views.

https://t.co/e8jVB7JgE9

items.title: Sometimes Silicon Valley companies do things that make me cry inside. One, a big company that everyo...
items.content:
Sometimes Silicon Valley companies do things that make me cry inside. One, a big company that everyone here knows laid off a large number of its R&D engineers and didn't pay any severance, or anything. Just said "your last day is today, goodbye." 

Corporations can be so cruel sometimes. 

I won't share the name, the guy who told me this is trying to find a job and doesn't want to destroy those efforts to work with other people, but this is horrible behavior. 

I know it happens all the time, but what happened to "treat people the way I'd like to be treated?" 

Makes me feel blessed that @Broadcom did the opposite when it laid off my wife, went the complete other direction. 

Which is why I will recommend Broadcom to people who ask "should I take a job there?"
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes a well-known big tech company that laid off R&D engineers with zero severance, contrasting it with Broadcom's more humane approach to layoffs.
items.reasoning: Interesting anecdote about tech layoff practices and corporate culture, but the unnamed company limits actionability. ~20K views and 132 likes show decent engagement. Not AI-specific but relevant to tech industry labor conditions.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer describes a well-known big tech company that laid off R&D engineers with zero severance, contrasting it with Broadcom's more humane approach to layoffs.
items.content_html:
Sometimes Silicon Valley companies do things that make me cry inside. One, a big company that everyone here knows laid off a large number of its R&D engineers and didn't pay any severance, or anything. Just said "your last day is today, goodbye."

Corporations can be so cruel sometimes.

I won't share the name, the guy who told me this is trying to find a job and doesn't want to destroy those efforts to work with other people, but this is horrible behavior.

I know it happens all the time, but what happened to "treat people the way I'd like to be treated?"

Makes me feel blessed that @Broadcom did the opposite when it laid off my wife, went the complete other direction.

Which is why I will recommend Broadcom to people who ask "should I take a job there?"

items.title:
Rakesh said something that stuck with me:

“AI infrastructure is about building a massive computer w...
items.content:
Rakesh said something that stuck with me:

“AI infrastructure is about building a massive computer with huge amounts of network bandwidth.”

Read that again.

AI isn’t just compute.

It’s:

→ Extreme east-west traffic
→ Massive bandwidth demands
→ Tight coupling between silicon, optics, systems, and orchestration

If your network is an afterthought, your AI strategy is too.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon quoting Cisco SVP about AI infrastructure requiring massive network bandwidth, not just compute.
items.reasoning: Insightful point that AI infrastructure is about networking as much as compute. Challenges common misconceptions, though sponsored.

items.title:
I've been using GitHub Copilot since it came out.

There are two areas I'd recommend developers look...
items.content:
I've been using GitHub Copilot since it came out.

There are two areas I'd recommend developers look into:

1. USING AI AS A COPILOT

GitHub Copilot fills this role. I drive, and GitHub Copilot autocompletes, suggests, and verifies. I stay 100% in control while pair-programming with it.

If you are a Visual Studio Code user, this one is a no-brainer.

Here is what I love about GitHub Copilot:

It handles everything I need: spec-driven development, documentation generation, testing, and I can use it with any model I want (including open-source models).

There's nothing to rethink or configure. I just turn it on and start coding.

2. ACTING AS A COPILOT TO AI

The GitHub Copilot CLI fills this role. You can plan and verify, while the agent refines, breaks down tasks, and executes.

That's all you need.

Two different modes. Two different purposes.

items.title:
The other big shift?

Owning and unifying the entire stack.

Enterprises don’t need another science ...
items.content:
The other big shift?

Owning and unifying the entire stack.

Enterprises don’t need another science experiment.
They need the easy button to operationalize AI.

That means:

→ Silicon
→ Optics
→ Systems
→ Orchestration layers

Working as one cohesive platform, not stitched together across 5 vendors and 10 roadmaps.

Fragmentation kills momentum.

items.title:
The real “aha” for me:

We are moving from prompting… to managing outcomes.

Instead of crafting the...
items.content:
The real “aha” for me:

We are moving from prompting… to managing outcomes.

Instead of crafting the perfect input, you:

→ Define the objective
→ Review the execution plan
→ Approve or refine

That’s not a better chatbot.

That’s a digital coworker.

And it changes how leaders design workflows.

"AI Ag...
items.content:
See the latest book from @AltoValentina, released by @PacktDataML at https://t.co/yPRfXTozGF

"AI Agents in Practice — Design, Implement, and Scale Autonomous #AI Systems for Production"

Book Description (from Amazon):

As AI agents evolve to take on complex tasks and operate autonomously, you need to learn how to build these next-generation systems. Author Valentina Alto brings practical, industry-grounded expertise in AI Agents in Practice to help you go beyond simple chatbots and create AI agents that plan, reason, collaborate, and solve real-world problems using large language models (LLMs) and the latest open-source frameworks.

In this book, you'll get a comparative tour of leading AI agent frameworks such as LangChain and LangGraph, covering each tool's strengths, ideal use cases, and how to apply them in real-world projects. Through step-by-step examples, you’ll learn how to construct single-agent and multi-agent architectures using proven design patterns to orchestrate AI agents working together. Case studies across industries will show you how AI agents drive value in real-world scenarios, while guidance on responsible AI will help you implement ethical guardrails from day one. The chapters also set the stage with a brief history of AI agents, from early rule-based systems to today's LLM-driven autonomous agents, so you understand how we got here and where the field is headed.

By the end of this book, you'll have the practical skills, design insights, and ethical foresight to build and deploy AI agents that truly make an impact.

items.content:
A new mini-app on Llambada: Chat with a PDF
https://t.co/VZ8OX2f0mv

This mini-app allows you to upload any PDF and chat with it. The mini-app implements a RAG approach.

The fun part is that as any mini-app on the platform, this one allows you to customize it for your own preferences. You can add the support of more than one PDF, add the support of Word or LaTeX projects, etc, simply by forking the mini-app and asking for a change in the chat.

C...
items.content:
@joshmatz @carlvellotti It’s slower because it’s not just “upload file, ask question, get answer”

Cowork requires giving access to a folder, copy file to that folder, ask question, it generates a plan, etc.

Not a big deal for one file. Very annoying when you are working with many files or making changes to a file.

It can be set up, but wouldn’t it be better if the chat supported this?

items.title:
We’ve been stuck in “chatbot mode.”

→ Ask a question
→ Get a response
→ Manually do the work

That’...
items.content:
We’ve been stuck in “chatbot mode.”

→ Ask a question
→ Get a response
→ Manually do the work

That’s augmentation.

Agents are different.

You define a goal.

It:
→ Scans your local folders
 → Detects duplicates
 → Proposes categories
 → Suggests naming conventions
 → Flags outdated files

Then it shows you the plan and waits for approval.

That shift is massive.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon contrasts chatbot mode (ask-respond-manually-do) with agent mode (define goal, agent scans/detects/proposes/flags, shows plan, waits for approval).
items.reasoning: Clear articulation of the chatbot-to-agent paradigm shift, part of thread.
items.summary_html: Van Loon contrasts chatbot mode (ask-respond-manually-do) with agent mode (define goal, agent scans/detects/proposes/flags, shows plan, waits for approval).
items.content_html:
We’ve been stuck in “chatbot mode.”

→ Ask a question

→ Get a response

→ Manually do the work

That’s augmentation.

Agents are different.

You define a goal.

It:

→ Scans your local folders

→ Detects duplicates

→ Proposes categories

→ Suggests naming conventions

→ Flags outdated files

Then it shows you the plan and waits for approval.

That shift is massive.

items.content:
And it doesn’t stop at file cleanup.

Think bigger:

→ Build spreadsheets from raw data
→ Prepare structured reports
→ Summarize messy notes
→ Pull context from Slack or your browser session

It breaks complex tasks into steps.

Works through them autonomously.

Keeps you in the loop.

This is delegation with guardrails.

items.title: Successful ideas scale with volume. You can’t reliably predict which one will land, even if you’re g...
items.content:
Successful ideas scale with volume. You can’t reliably predict which one will land, even if you’re great. Masterpiece and failure come from the same process.

So maximize attempts. Make failure cheap. Lower the barrier to experimenting. More shots. Better odds.

If you can't predict success, you must maximize attempts. The more work you produce, the more opportunities you create for greatness.
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Camilo Valenzuela shares philosophy on maximizing creative attempts: success is unpredictable so you must maximize shots.
items.reasoning: General productivity/creative philosophy, loosely relevant to AI-era building but not AI-specific.
items.summary_html: Camilo Valenzuela shares philosophy on maximizing creative attempts: success is unpredictable so you must maximize shots.

items.title: Highly rated book from @PacktDataML provides a great Guide to using OpenSearch and its many applicat...
items.content:
Highly rated book from @PacktDataML provides a great Guide to using OpenSearch and its many applications: https://t.co/354NCwCGRp
 ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ 
𝙂𝙤𝙤𝙜𝙡𝙚 𝙨𝙖𝙮𝙨: "OpenSearch is an open-source search and analytics suite that provides scalable full-text search, real-time application monitoring, log analytics, security analytics, and vector database capabilities. Derived from Elasticsearch as a fork, it offers a community-driven, Apache 2.0-licensed solution for data-intensive applications, along with OpenSearch Dashboards for data visualization and exploration. OpenSearch is known for its high availability, performance, and flexibility, allowing users to analyze, visualize, and build data-driven applications across various use cases."
★ ★ ★ ★ ★

𝓑𝓸𝓸𝓴  𝓓𝓮𝓼𝓬𝓻𝓲𝓹𝓽𝓲𝓸𝓷:
From seasoned data professionals managing billions of records to aspiring analysts exploring diverse datasets, this guide is for users at all levels who want to make the most of OpenSearch's capabilities and functionalities. Written by distinguished AWS Solutions Architects Jon Handler, Ph.D., a former search engine developer, Prashant Agrawal, a search specialist, and Soujanya Konka, an expert in large-scale data migrations, this guide brings together deep technical expertise with practical, hands-on knowledge of implementing OpenSearch in real-world scenarios.

Starting with an introduction to OpenSearch, you’ll get to grips with the key features before delving into essential topics such as installing OpenSearch, ingesting data, crafting queries, visualizing results, ensuring security, and optimizing performance. Each concept is accompanied by practical examples and tutorials, allowing you to grasp the material through hands-on experience.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne promoting an OpenSearch guide book with detailed description.
items.reasoning: Book promotion. OpenSearch has vector database capabilities relevant to AI but post is purely promotional.

Derived from Elasticsearch as a fork, it offers a community-driven, Apache 2.0-licensed solution for data-intensive applications, along with OpenSearch Dashboards for data visualization and exploration. ★ ★ ★ ★ ★

𝓑𝓸𝓸𝓴 𝓓𝓮𝓼𝓬𝓻𝓲𝓹𝓽𝓲𝓸𝓷:

From seasoned data professionals managing billions of records to aspiring analysts exploring diverse datasets, this guide is for users at all levels who want to make the most of OpenSearch's capabilities and functionalities. Written by distinguished AWS Solutions Architects Jon Handler, Ph.D., a former search engine developer, Prashant Agrawal, a search specialist, and Soujanya Konka, an expert in large-scale data migrations, this guide brings together deep technical expertise with practical, hands-on knowledge of implementing OpenSearch in real-world scenarios.

Starting with an introduction to OpenSearch, you’ll get to grips with the key features before delving into essential topics such as installing OpenSearch, ingesting data, crafting queries, visualizing results, ensuring security, and optimizing performance. Each concept is accompanied by practical examples and tutorials, allowing you to grasp the material through hands-on experience.

items.title: I tried the “Pete and Bobby Challenge” of 100 push-ups and 50 pull ups in 10 minutes. The push ups a...
items.content: I tried the “Pete and Bobby Challenge” of 100 push-ups and 50 pull ups in 10 minutes. The push ups aren’t a problem, and I figured I could do 5 pull ups x 10 on a 1 minute cadence, but I crapped out after 25. It turns out I can barely do 5x10 on a two minute cadence, so I have a ways to go.
